screens.title: TMUA index questions are rarely just index questions
screens.body: In the TMUA, index laws often appear inside a larger problem. You may need to rewrite roots, split fractions, or simplify powers before you can differentiate, compare values, or spot a structure.
screens.keyPoint: Useful rewrites: √x = x1/2, x√x = x3/2, and 1xn = x-n.

screens.title: Rewrite before you manipulate

steps.title: Start with the denominator
steps.body: If you see x√x, rewrite it as x × x<sup>1/2</sup> = x<sup>3/2</sup>.

steps.title: Split the fraction
steps.body: x2 - 4x√x becomes x2x3/2 - 4x3/2.

steps.title: Subtract powers when dividing
steps.body: x<sup>2</sup> ÷ x<sup>3/2</sup> = x<sup>1/2</sup>, and 4 ÷ x<sup>3/2</sup> = 4x<sup>-3/2</sup>.

steps.title: Final simplified form
steps.body: So x2 - 4x√x = x1/2 - 4x-3/2.

screens.question: For x > 0, which expression is equal to x2 - 4x√x?
screens.prompt: Rewrite the denominator as a single power of x.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. x√x = x<sup>3/2</sup>, so the expression becomes x<sup>2-3/2</sup> - 4x<sup>-3/2</sup> = x<sup>1/2</sup> - 4x<sup>-3/2</sup>.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. First rewrite x√x as x<sup>3/2</sup>. Then split the fraction: x<sup>2</sup> ÷ x<sup>3/2</sup> = x<sup>1/2</sup>, and 4 ÷ x<sup>3/2</sup> = 4x<sup>-3/2</sup>.

screens.title: Indices handled.
screens.body: You have taken a messy expression, rewritten it with powers, and simplified it term by term. Next up: surds.

screens.title: Surds are exact values, not decimals
screens.body: In TMUA questions, surds are often used because they keep values exact. You should be comfortable simplifying surds, combining like surds, and rationalising denominators without reaching for a calculator.
screens.keyPoint: A common move is taking out square factors: √72 = √(36 × 2) = 6√2. You can only combine like surds, so 3√2 + 5√2 = 8√2, but √2 + √3 cannot be simplified.

screens.title: Rationalising the denominator

steps.title: The aim
steps.body: Rationalising means rewriting a fraction so there is no surd left in the denominator.

steps.title: Simple case
steps.body: For 5√3, multiply the numerator and denominator by √3.

steps.title: Complete the multiplication
steps.body: 5√3 × √3√3 = 5√33, because √3 × √3 = 3.

steps.title: Two-term denominator
steps.body: If the denominator has two terms, such as 2 + √3, multiply by its conjugate, 2 - √3.

screens.question: Which expression is equal to 62 + √3?
screens.prompt: Multiply the numerator and denominator by the conjugate 2 - √3.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Multiplying by 2 - √32 - √3 gives denominator (2 + √3)(2 - √3) = 1, while the numerator becomes 6(2 - √3) = 12 - 6√3.
screens.incorrectFeedback: The conjugate of 2 + √3 is 2 - √3. The denominator becomes 4 - 3 = 1, while the numerator becomes 6(2 - √3) = 12 - 6√3.

screens.title: Surds done.
screens.body: You have practised exact surd manipulation and rationalising denominators. Next up, and finally for this section: polynomials.

screens.title: Polynomials are about structure
screens.body: For TMUA algebra, polynomials often appear with hidden information: a known factor, a remainder, an unknown constant, or a root. The key is knowing what substitution tells you.
screens.keyPoint: If a polynomial f(x) is divided by x - a, the remainder is f(a). If f(a) = 0, then x - a is a factor.

screens.title: Remainder theorem and factor theorem

steps.title: Start with the divisor
steps.body: If a polynomial is divided by x - a, substitute x = a into the polynomial.

steps.title: Remainder theorem
steps.body: The remainder when f(x) is divided by x - a is f(a). For example, if dividing by x - 2, the remainder is f(2).

steps.title: Factor theorem
steps.body: The factor theorem is a specific case of the remainder theorem. If f(a) = 0, the remainder is zero, so x - a is a factor.

steps.title: Unknown constants
steps.body: If a question says the remainder is 7, set f(a) = 7. If it says x - a is a factor, set f(a) = 0.

screens.title: Indices, Surds and Polynomials: key points
keyPoints: Rewrite roots and denominators using powers when it makes algebra easier.
Split algebraic fractions before simplifying term by term.
Surds are exact values and should often be simplified rather than decimalised.
To rationalise a denominator with two terms, multiply by the conjugate.
The remainder when f(x) is divided by x - a is f(a).
The factor theorem is the zero-remainder case of the remainder theorem.
reviewItems: Practise simplifying expressions involving x√x and negative powers.
Practise using f(a) to handle polynomial remainders and factors.
Next lesson: exponential equations and hidden quadratics.

screens.title: The discriminant tells us about the roots.
screens.body: The expression b² − 4ac determines how many real roots a quadratic has.

screens.title: Sequences can be described in different ways
screens.body: A sequence is an ordered list of terms. It may be defined by a formula for its nth term, or generated one term at a time using a recurrence relation.
screens.keyPoint: A sequence lists terms, such as 3, 7, 11, 15, ... A series is formed when those terms are added: 3 + 7 + 11 + 15 + ...

screens.title: Nth-term formulae and recurrence relations

steps.title: Using an nth-term formula
steps.body: If u<sub>n</sub> = 4n - 1, substitute the term number directly. For example, u<sub>10</sub> = 4(10) - 1 = 39.

steps.title: Using a recurrence relation
steps.body: A recurrence relation builds each term from a previous term. It must include a starting value.

steps.title: Start with the given term
steps.body: If x<sub>1</sub> = 2 and x<sub>n+1</sub> = 3x<sub>n</sub> - 1, then x<sub>2</sub> = 3(2) - 1 = 5.

steps.title: Continue one term at a time
steps.body: The next terms are x<sub>3</sub> = 3(5) - 1 = 14 and x<sub>4</sub> = 3(14) - 1 = 41.

screens.question: A sequence is defined by x<sub>1</sub> = 2 and x<sub>n+1</sub> = 3x<sub>n</sub> - 1. What is x<sub>4</sub>?
screens.prompt: Use each new term to calculate the next one.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. The sequence begins 2, 5, 14, 41, so x<sub>4</sub> = 41.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Work forwards carefully: x<sub>2</sub> = 5, x<sub>3</sub> = 14 and x<sub>4</sub> = 3(14) - 1 = 41.

screens.title: Arithmetic progressions have a constant difference
screens.body: In an arithmetic progression, the same value is added to move from one term to the next. This value is called the common difference and is written as d.
screens.keyPoint: If the first term is a and the common difference is d, then u<sub>n</sub> = a + (n - 1)d.

screens.title: Find a term in an arithmetic progression

steps.title: Identify the first term
steps.body: For 12, 7, 2, -3, ... the first term is a = 12.

steps.title: Find the common difference
steps.body: Each term is 5 less than the previous term, so d = -5.

screens.question: The sequence 12, 7, 2, -3, ... is arithmetic. What is its fifteenth term?
screens.prompt: There are 14 common differences between the first and fifteenth terms.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Use u<sub>n</sub> = a + (n - 1)d with a = 12, n = 15 and d = -5. This gives -58.

screens.title: Arithmetic series add the terms of an arithmetic progression
screens.body: The sum of the first n terms is written as S<sub>n</sub>. It can be calculated using the first term and common difference, or using the first and last terms.
screens.keyPoint: Sn = n2[2a + (n - 1)d] = n2(a + l), where l is the final term.

screens.title: The sum of the first n natural numbers

steps.title: Recognise the arithmetic series
steps.body: The series 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + n has first term 1 and final term n.

steps.title: Use the first-and-last-term formula
steps.body: Sn = n2(1 + n).

steps.title: Write the standard result
steps.body: Therefore 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + n = n(n + 1)2.

steps.title: Example
steps.body: The sum of the first 20 natural numbers is 20 × 212 = 210.

screens.question: Find the sum of the first 12 terms of 5, 8, 11, 14, ...

screens.title: Geometric progressions have a constant ratio
screens.body: In a geometric progression, each term is multiplied by the same value to obtain the next term. This value is called the common ratio and is written as r.
screens.keyPoint: If the first term is a and the common ratio is r, then u<sub>n</sub> = ar<sup>n-1</sup>.

screens.title: Find the common ratio and nth term

steps.title: Divide consecutive terms
steps.body: For 81, -27, 9, -3, ... divide -27 by 81 to obtain r = -1/3.

steps.title: Write the nth term
steps.body: Since a = 81, the nth term is u<sub>n</sub> = 81(-1/3)<sup>n-1</sup>.

steps.title: Interpret the negative ratio
steps.body: A negative common ratio makes the signs alternate between positive and negative.

steps.title: Interpret its magnitude
steps.body: Because |r| is less than 1, the magnitude of the terms becomes smaller.

screens.title: A finite geometric series has a fixed number of terms
screens.body: The first n terms of a geometric progression can be added using the finite geometric-series formula. This lesson only considers finite sums; sums to infinity will be covered later.
screens.keyPoint: For r ≠ 1, Sn = a(1 - rn)1 - r. For example, 3 + 6 + 12 + 24 + 48 = 3(1 - 25)/(1 - 2) = 93.

screens.question: Find the sum 2 + 6 + 18 + 54 + 162.
screens.prompt: Identify a, r and n before using the finite geometric-series formula.
screens.correctFeedback: Correct. Here a = 2, r = 3 and n = 5, so S<sub>5</sub> = 2(1 - 3<sup>5</sup>)/(1 - 3) = 242.
screens.incorrectFeedback: Here a = 2, r = 3 and n = 5. Substituting into the finite geometric-series formula gives 242.

screens.title: Arithmetic and geometric progressions: key points
keyPoints: An nth-term formula lets you calculate any term directly.
A recurrence relation requires a starting value and builds each term from a previous term.
An arithmetic progression has a constant difference.
For an arithmetic progression, u<sub>n</sub> = a + (n - 1)d.
For an arithmetic series, S<sub>n</sub> = n/2[2a + (n - 1)d].
The sum of the first n natural numbers is n(n + 1)/2.
A geometric progression has a constant ratio.
For a geometric progression, u<sub>n</sub> = ar<sup>n-1</sup>.
For a finite geometric series, S<sub>n</sub> = a(1 - r<sup>n</sup>)/(1 - r).
Sums to infinity are not included in this lesson.
reviewItems: Practise generating terms from nth-term formulae and recurrence relations.
Check whether consecutive terms have a constant difference or constant ratio.
Use the correct formula for arithmetic and finite geometric sums.
Next lesson: binomial expansion and factorials.

screens.question: Adding Log(A) and Log(B) gives Log(AB)
screens.incorrectFeedback: When adding two logarithms of the same base, you can simply take the logarithm of the product
